skill.name: jeo
skill.description: JEO — Integrated AI agent orchestration skill. Plan with ralph+plannotator, execute with team/bmad, verify browser behavior with agent-browser, apply UI feedback with agentation(annotate), auto-cleanup worktrees after completion. Supports Claude, Codex, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode. Install: ralph, omc, omx, ohmg, bmad, plannotator, agent-browser, agentation.
skill.summary: Unified AI agent orchestration for multi-agent workflows with visual plan approval and UI feedback loops
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: All 1127 static findings evaluated as false positives. This is a legitimate AI agent orchestration skill that coordinates planning, execution, verification, and cleanup phases. Commands are hardcoded or use trusted tooling from official sources. Network access is limited to package installation and localhost agentation communication. Filesystem operations are confined to project directories and standard config paths. No malicious intent or data exfiltration patterns detected.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used for Session Isolation Only
low_findings.description: MD5 hashing is used exclusively for generating unique session/temp directory names in /tmp (e.g., hashlib.md5(os.getcwd().encode()).hexdigest()[:8]). This is not a cryptographic security concern as MD5 is not used for security-sensitive operations. SHA-256 is correctly used for plan hash comparison.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed through code analysis: MD5 generates session keys for /tmp directory isolation, not for security. SHA-256 is used for plan integrity checks.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution in Setup Scripts
low_findings.description: Command substitution syntax (e.g., $(), backticks) is used throughout setup scripts for variable expansion and script path resolution. All commands are hardcoded or use trusted tooling paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Command substitution is standard shell scripting pattern used for path resolution and environment variable access. No user input flows into these commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell for Package Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged pipe-to-shell patterns (curl | bash) for installing bun and plannotator. EVALUATION: These are official installation methods from trusted sources (bun.sh, plannotator.ai). While not best practice, this is standard for these tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are official install scripts from trusted sources (bun.sh, plannotator.ai). Pattern is standard industry practice for these tools.

dangerous_patterns.title: Home Directory File Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged access to hidden files in home directory (~/.bashrc, ~/.claude/settings.json, etc.). EVALUATION: These are standard setup scripts that configure the user's development environment by modifying config files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Setup scripts routinely access ~/.bashrc, ~/.config, and tool-specific config files. This is legitimate tooling behavior, not a security concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reference Documentation Path Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 'rm -rf /path/to/worktree' as recursive delete on root/home. EVALUATION: This is documentation showing how to manually remove a worktree - the path is a placeholder, not an actual root/home path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path '/path/to/worktree' is clearly a documentation placeholder. The actual worktree-cleanup.sh script uses git worktree commands with proper safeguards.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.check_output for Git Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: Python subprocess calls are used throughout for git operations, state file management, and running trusted tools. All arguments are hardcoded or derived from safe sources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: subprocess usage is for git operations, state file management, and running approved tools. Arguments are hardcoded or derived from safe project state files.

content.user_title: Automate full AI agent workflows with JEO orchestration
content.value_statement: Managing multi-agent development workflows across Claude, Codex, Gemini, and OpenCode requires coordinating planning, execution, verification, and cleanup phases. JEO provides unified orchestration with visual plan approval, browser verification, and automatic worktree cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Plan tasks with ralph+plannotator for visual plan review and approval before coding
Execute parallel agent workflows via team (Claude) or bmad orchestrator (other platforms)
Verify browser-based behavior automatically using agent-browser snapshots
Process UI feedback loops with agentation for annotation-based review cycles
Clean up git worktrees automatically after task completion
Coordinate the same workflow across Claude, Codex, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode
limitations: Requires installation of dependencies: ralph, plannotator, agent-browser, agentation, and optional omc/omx/ohmg
Browser verification requires a running dev server and localhost accessibility
UI annotation feedback requires Agentation React component to be mounted in the project

use_cases.title: Complex multi-agent task orchestration
use_cases.description: DevOps teams managing complex features can use JEO to plan with visual approval, execute parallel agents, verify browser behavior, and automatically clean up worktrees. The plan hash prevents re-reviewing unchanged plans.
use_cases.target_user: Development teams building complex features requiring multiple AI agents

use_cases.title: Visual UI feedback loops
use_cases.description: Frontend developers can trigger UI annotation review by saying 'annotate'. The agent waits for explicit submission, then processes annotations, fixes issues, and re-verifies browser rendering.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers needing visual review and feedback on UI implementations

use_cases.title: Cross-platform AI tool coordination
use_cases.description: Users working with multiple AI coding tools can use the same JEO workflow across Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode. Setup scripts configure each platform's specific integration.
use_cases.target_user: Developers using multiple AI coding tools across different projects

prompt_templates.title: Start full JEO workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Activate full JEO orchestration with plan, execute, verify, and cleanup phases

prompt_templates.title: Plan-only with visual review
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan this feature: 'add dark mode toggle to settings page'. Show me the plan for approval before coding.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use ralph+plannotator for visual plan review without immediate execution

prompt_templates.title: UI annotation review
prompt_templates.prompt: annotate - review the current UI for accessibility issues and report any annotations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Trigger UI feedback loop with agentation for annotation-based review

prompt_templates.title: Resume from checkpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: jeo - resume from previous checkpoint. Check the state file and continue where we left off.
prompt_templates.scenario: Resume interrupted workflow using persisted state

output_examples.output:

Planning with ralph+plannotator...
- Plan hash: a1b2c3d4...
- Review status: awaiting approval
- Plan details saved to .omc/state/jeo-state.json

Awaiting visual approval via plannotator...

output_examples.input: After plan approval, agent executes the task
output_examples.output:
Plan approved. Transitioning to EXECUTE phase.

Team available: true
Invoking /omc:team 3:executor 'add search functionality to dashboard'

Parallel agents deployed: [analysis, planning, solutioning, implementation]
Execution complete.

best_practices: Review plans visually with ralph+plannotator before coding starts to catch direction errors early
Use the annotate keyword when UI verification is needed - it properly gates on explicit submission
Let the plan hash mechanism prevent unnecessary re-reviews when plan content has not changed
anti_patterns: Do not skip the PLAN phase for complex features - plan approval prevents wasted execution
Do not manually edit jeo-state.json during active workflows - use the documented checkpoint mechanism
Do not run multiple JEO workflows in parallel on the same repository without worktree isolation

faq.question: What AI tools does JEO support?
faq.answer: JEO supports Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode. Each platform has specific setup scripts to configure the JEO hooks and integration.

faq.question: How does the plan hash prevent unnecessary reviews?
faq.answer: JEO computes SHA-256 hash of plan.md content. If the same hash already has terminal status (approved/feedback_required), the plan gate stays closed until plan content changes.

faq.question: What is the annotate keyword for?
faq.answer: The annotate keyword triggers UI feedback mode. It waits for explicit user submission via Send Annotations before processing annotations. This prevents premature interruption of execution.

faq.question: How does worktree cleanup work?
faq.answer: JEO runs worktree-cleanup.sh after task completion. It only removes git worktrees, not the main repository. Files created outside worktrees are preserved.

faq.question: What dependencies need to be installed?
faq.answer: Required: ralph, plannotator, agent-browser. Optional: omc, omx, ohmg, bmad, agentation. Run scripts/install.sh --all to install everything.

faq.question: How does JEO coordinate across platforms?
faq.answer: JEO uses a state file (.omc/state/jeo-state.json) that each platform reads and writes. This provides consistent state across Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, and OpenCode sessions.
